# EMEA data centre vacancy hits record low as AI demand outpaces supply

April 10, 2026

Share

The EMEA data centre market is tightening rapidly, with demand continuing to outpace supply across many of its most established hubs.

That’s according to recent data from JLL, which showed vacancy rates at the end of 2025 across the FLAP-D markets of Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin fell to a record low of 6.3% in Q4 2025, down sharply from 16.9% in 2021.

At the same time as the record-low vacancy rates, the data showed that 83% of the pipeline is already pre-let, underlining just how difficult it is becoming for occupiers to secure meaningful capacity in Europe’s core colocation markets.

It’s not exactly surprising to see vacancy rates hit record lows, as the demand for AI continues to dominate the market. That’s one of the key reasons we’re seeing more proposals for new build data centres – especially as many legacy facilities aren’t capable of supplying the capacity that is needed by some of the larger AI companies that are scrambling for space"

# Data centre vacancy hits record low as demand outstrips supply

News

by

Akanksha Soni

09 April 2026

Data centres, Industrial

Vacancy rates across Europe’s core data centre markets have fallen to a record low as demand continues to outpace supply, according to JLL.

Across the FLAP-D markets – Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin – combined live capacity has doubled from 1.8GW in 2019 to 3.6GW by the end of 2025, despite ongoing development and grid constraints.

But that growth has failed to keep pace with demand. Colocation vacancy has dropped sharply from 16.9% in 2021 to just 6.3% in Q4 2025, leaving availability at effectively constrained levels.

JLL said capacity is now being absorbed faster than it can be delivered, with around 83% of space currently under construction already pre-let, underlining the depth of occupier demand.

This tightening supply-demand dynamic is reshaping how occupiers access space, with pre-leasing now the primary route to securing capacity.

London and Frankfurt have emerged as the main beneficiaries of supply constraints elsewhere, absorbing demand that may otherwise have been directed to other European hubs.

London alone has around 302MW in its development pipeline, but availability remains limited, with large occupiers increasingly forced to commit to space well ahead of delivery.

The report also highlights a shift in Dubl"

### Demand is outpacing new supply across the EMEA data centre market

The EMEA data centre market entered 2026 in a state of structural undersupply, with demand for capacity rising faster than new projects can be delivered. In its year-end 2025 report, JLL says the region has reached a pivotal moment shaped by unprecedented demand, rapid AI adoption and a broader geographical rebalancing of new development. The firm’s central conclusion is clear: capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced, turning access to power and space into the defining issue for operators, cloud platforms and enterprise occupiers.

That tension is especially visible in Europe’s FLAP-D markets — Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin. JLL says combined live capacity across those five markets rose from 1.8 GW in 2019 to 3.6 GW in 2025, effectively doubling in six years. Yet even that expansion has not kept pace with new demand because mature markets are being constrained by grid limitations, regulatory friction and the scarcity of sites with viable power access.

### Long-Lead Equipment Risk Reference Hyperscale Data Center Programs | Q1–Q2 2026

On hyperscale data center programs, the Ready-for-Service date is not determined by structural steel or MEP rough-in. It is determined by the procurement decisions made — or deferred — in the first ninety days of the project. This reference identifies the equipment categories most likely to slip a schedule, ranks them by combined risk exposure, and documents the planning errors that convert long lead times into missed energization dates.

TIER 1

RFS-Critical | 52–128+ week lead times

TIER 2

High Risk | 40–80 week lead times

TIER 3

Elevated Risk | 20–52 week lead times

## Equipment Risk Summary Table

Lead times shown are current market estimates as of Q1–Q2 2026. Ranges reflect variation by manufacturer, geography, voltage class, and custom specification requirements. All lead times should be confirmed against vendor-specific manufacturing slot availability at time of submittal, not catalog estimates.

    "text": "Data Center Lead Times: PM Guide

June 21, 2026

# How Data Center Construction Project Managers Manage Equipment Lead Times

By:

If long-lead gear is not tied to the schedule on day one, the project can miss power-on, testing, and go-live by months. I’d sum it up like this: start procurement early, track every approval and factory date, line delivery up with commissioning, and keep backup supply options ready before a vendor slips.

Here’s the short version:

- I build the schedule around equipment lead times, not around best-case guesses.
- I split each package into steps like submittals, approvals, fabrication, FAT, shipping, and startup.
- I track the items most likely to control the job, including switchgear, transformers, generators, UPS systems, chillers, and AHUs/CRACs.
- I watch the dates that matter most: release dates, required on-site dates, FAT windows, and commissioning use dates.
- I push long-lead submittals early because late design changes can add 4 to 8 weeks.
- I keep vendors on a set reporting cycle and act fast if forecast delivery moves.
- I line procurement up with SAT, IST, and turnover paperwork so gear is not just delivered, but ready to use.

A few numbers make the point fast: MV switchgear can run 52 to 65 weeks, transformers 60+ weeks, and IST often takes 6 to 12 weeks and does not shrink just because more labor shows up. On hyperscale work, a missed energization window can cost tens of millions of dollars per month.

# Data Center Electrical Procurement: Why the Order Has to Come Before the Design

Power transformer lead times from major manufacturers have reached 48-60 months in 2026, with Tier 1 suppliers booked through 2030. This post explains the five-stage electrical procurement workflow that works in today's market, from pre-LOI utility confirmation through continuous schedule monitoring, and the budget implications developers must model now.

by Build Team • June 22, 2026 • 5 min read

# Data Center Electrical Procurement: Why the Order Has to Come Before the Design

Transformer lead times now reach 60 months from Tier 1 suppliers. Developers who sequence procurement after design lock are building on borrowed time.

The conventional development sequence treats electrical equipment procurement as a downstream task: finish design, secure permits, then buy the gear. For data centers in 2026, that sequence is obsolete. Power transformer lead times from major manufacturers have reached 48 to 60 months. Generator step-up transformers average 144 weeks. Tier 1 suppliers -- ABB, Siemens Energy, Hitachi Energy -- have backlogs extending to 2030 and beyond.

A developer who sequences procurement conventionally and targets energization in 2028 has already missed the window.

# Data Center Transformer Procurement in 2026: Why Electrical Equipment Is Now a Development Constraint

This post explains why transformer and switchgear procurement has become a major constraint for data center development in 2026. It covers lead times, utility coordination, capital planning and how AI helps teams track equipment risk across site selection, procurement and delivery.

by Build Team • May 26, 2026 • 5 min read

# Data Center Transformer Procurement in 2026: Why Electrical Equipment Is Now a Development Constraint

Transformer and switchgear procurement has moved from purchasing detail to critical-path development risk.

Data center transformer procurement is now one of the earliest go/no-go questions in development. A site can have land control, zoning support and tenant demand, but still fail if the electrical equipment timeline does not match the energization date.

The constraint is structural. Data center load growth, grid upgrades, renewable interconnection, industrial electrification and utility replacement cycles are all competing for the same transformer, switchgear and breaker capacity. Reuters reported that U.S. power transformer buyers are scrambling for imports and factory slots. pv magazine USA reported that tr"

    "title": "Electrical Switchgear Shortage: Supply Chain Impact & Solutions",
    "url": "https://feeds.deipower.com/blog/electrical-switchgear-shortage",
    "publishedDate": "2026-05-06T01:00:47.000Z",
    "text": "May 6, 2026

## Introduction

Electrical switchgear shortages are now stalling projects nationwide and forcing contractors, engineers, and facility teams to rethink how they procure critical power distribution equipment. Switchgear that once shipped in weeks now takes months—or even years—creating cascading delays across data centers, healthcare facilities, industrial plants, and utility infrastructure projects. Medium-voltage switchgear lead times currently sit at 40-80 weeks, more than double pre-pandemic averages, while large low-voltage switchboards lag at 35-62 weeks compared to the 20-30 weeks once considered standard.

Those delays have real consequences. Projects that still treat switchgear as a routine, late-stage procurement item are absorbing the full force of the shortage: collapsed commissioning windows, extended temporary power arrangements, and budget overruns running into six or seven figures.

The root causes—surging demand from data centers and grid modernization, copper price volatility, and ongoing regulatory transitions—are well-documented. What's less clear for many project teams is how to respond before the delays hit.

## Key Takeaways

- Switchgear lead times have ballooned to 40–80 weeks industry-wide, up from 24–34 weeks pre-COVID
- The shortage stems from four converging pressures: data center demand, grid modernization, copper supply constraints, and SF6 regulatory transitions
- Projects that treat switchgear as a late-stage procurement item face "

Pre-commitment is now the only viable route to securing meaningful capacity across Europe."},{"url":"https://datacentrereview.com/2026/04/emea-data-centre-vacancy-hits-record-low-as-ai-demand-outpaces-supply/","stance":"supports","quote":"vacancy rates at the end of 2025 across the FLAP-D markets of Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin fell to a record low of 6.3% in Q4 2025, down sharply from 16.9% in 2021. At the same time as the record-low vacancy rates, the data showed that 83% of the pipeline is already pre-let"},{"url":"https://www.estatesgazette.co.uk/news/data-centre-vacancy-hits-record-low-as-demand-outstrips-supply/","stance":"supports","quote":"around 83% of space currently under construction already pre-let, underlining the depth of occupier demand. This tightening supply-demand dynamic is reshaping how occupiers access space, with pre-leasing now the primary route to securing capacity."},{"url":"https://build.inc/insights/data-center-electrical-procurement-sequence-workflow","stance":"supports","quote":"The conventional development sequence treats electrical equipment procurement as a downstream task: finish design, secure permits, then buy the gear. For data centers in 2026, that sequence is obsolete. Power transformer lead times from major manufacturers have reached 48 to 60 months."},{"url":"https://feeds.deipower.com/blog/electrical-switchgear-shortage","stance":"supports","quote":"Medium-voltage switchgear lead times currently sit at 40-80 weeks, more than double pre-pandemic averages, while large low-voltage switchboards lag at 35-62 weeks compared to the 20-30 weeks once considered standard."},{"url":"https://gpuleaseindex.com/methodology/colocation-rfp-checklist","stance":"silent","quote":"Organizations are increasingly evaluating multiple providers and financing models. Key factors include equipment availability, power efficiency, cooling requirements, and total cost of ownership."}],"status":"corroborated","statusRationale":"Multiple independent sources (JLL via three outlets) confirm the trigger premise verbatim: FLAP-D vacancy at record-low 6.3% with 83% pre-let pipeline, and that pre-commitment is now the primary route to securing capacity. Long-lead procurement sources corroborate the mechanism's core assumption that electrical gear (switchgear 40-80 weeks, transformers 48-60 months) is specified and ordered well before energization, supporting the claim that 2026-27 builds had electrical BOM locked at pre-let. However, the sources speak to transformers/switchgear (facility-level long-lead items) more than to rack-level PDUs, which have shorter lead times and are more commonly procured closer to fit-out — a gap the hypothesis glosses. The falsifier query returned no evidence of open multi-bidder PDU bake-offs, but absence is not refutation. The key contestable-share leap (PDUs specifically locked at pre-let vs. procured later) remains partly inferential.","evidence":[{"title":"EMEA year end data centre report 2025","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"JLL","date":"2026-03-30","excerpt":"FLAP-D vacancy hits record low of 6.3%, pipeline is 83% pre-let. Capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced. Pre-commitment is now the only viable route to securing meaningful capacity across Europe.","url":"https://www.jll.com/en-uk/insights/emea-data-centre-report"},{"title":"Data Center Electrical Procurement: Why the Order Has to Come Before the Design","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"Build.inc","date":"2026-06-22","excerpt":"The conventional development sequence treats electrical equipment procurement as a downstream task... For data centers in 2026, that sequence is obsolete. Power transformer lead times from major manufacturers have reached 48 to 60 months.","url":"https://build.inc/insights/data-center-electrical-procurement-sequence-workflow"},{"title":"Data centre vacancy hits record low as demand outstrips supply","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"Estates Gazette","date":"2026-04-09","excerpt":"around 83% of space currently under construction already pre-let... pre-leasing now the primary route to securing capacity.","url":"https://www.estatesgazette.co.uk/news/data-centre-vacancy-hits-record-low-as-demand-outstrips-supply/"}],"revisedLikelihood":0.42,"revisionRationale":"Trigger and framework-timing premises are strongly corroborated by independent industry sources, raising confidence in the foreclosure narrative. But the load-bearing leap — that rack-level PDUs (shorter lead time, often procured near fit-out and subject to churn/expansion demand) are foreclosed to the same degree as facility long-lead electrical gear — is not established by the evidence, which concerns transformers and switchgear. This keeps the likelihood near the original, marginally lower to reflect the unaddressed PDU-vs-facility-gear distinction that the hypothesis itself concedes leaves retrofit/expansion demand open."}
